title: The Vim Grammar
subtitle: [count] [register] operator [count] {motion | text-object}
summary: Every editing command in Vim is one sentence: optional count, optional register, an operator (verb), then a motion or text object (noun). This is the grammar that makes Vim feel infinite from a small number of keys.

blocks.text: Most editors have *commands*: "delete word," "select to end of line," "comment block." Vim doesn't. Vim has a **grammar** — a small set of verbs and nouns that you compose into sentences. Once you see it, you stop memorizing keys.

blocks.title: The Grammar in One Line
blocks.text:
Every editing action in *normal mode* follows the form:

```
[count] ["register] operator [count] {motion | text-object}
```

All the brackets are optional. Concrete examples:

  • {key:d}{key:w} — delete one word (no count, default register, motion).
  • {key:5}{key:d}{key:w} — delete five words.
  • {key:"}{key:a}{key:5}{key:d}{key:w} — delete five words *into register a*.
  • {key:c}{key:i}{key:p} — change the inner paragraph (text object).
  • {key:>}{key:a}{key:{} — indent the brace block including its braces.
  • {key:g}{key:U}{key:t}{key:Z} — uppercase up to the next `Z`.

There is no command list to memorize. There are operators. There are motions and text objects. Multiplication is the count. The register is *where the result goes*.

blocks.title: The Operators
blocks.text:
The full operator set (the verbs):

  • {key:d} — delete (cut into a register).
  • {key:c} — change (delete + Insert).
  • {key:y} — yank (copy).
  • {key:>} {key:<} — indent / dedent.
  • {key:=} — re-indent (use language indenter).
  • {key:g}{key:u} {key:g}{key:U} {key:g}{key:~} — lowercase / uppercase / toggle case.
  • {key:g}{key:q} — reformat (text-wrap).
  • {key:!} — filter through external command.
  • {key:g}{key:@} — call user-defined `operatorfunc` (advanced).

That's it. ~10 verbs. Each one takes any motion or text object. Pair them up: that's the language.

blocks.title: Doubled Operators
blocks.text:
Every operator has a *doubled* form that means "act on the current line":

  • {key:d}{key:d} — delete line.
  • {key:c}{key:c} — change line.
  • {key:y}{key:y} — yank line.
  • {key:>}{key:>} {key:<}{key:<} — indent / dedent line.
  • {key:=}{key:=} — re-indent line.
  • {key:g}{key:U}{key:U} or {key:g}{key:U}{key:u} — uppercase line.

This is a special case in the grammar, not a separate command. The pattern *is* the rule: doubled operator = current line.

blocks.title: Synonyms (Single-Key Aliases)
Operator-Pending Is a Real Mode
blocks.text:
Between pressing the operator and pressing the motion or text object, Vim is in a fourth major mode: **operator-pending**. It's not a UI flourish — it's a named state in the editor's state machine, with its own keymap. Until a motion resolves, no buffer change has happened: the operator is staged with its count, the motion will produce `(start, end, type)`, and only then does the operator function execute.

The practical consequence is that op-pending has its *own* mapping table. {key::}{key:o}{key:n}{key:o}{key:r}{key:e}{key:m}{key:a}{key:p} (op-pending non-recursive map) defines mappings that *only fire after an operator*. So `:onoremap p i(` makes {key:d}{key:p} mean "delete inside parens" without breaking {key:p} (paste) in *normal mode*. This is the mechanism every custom text-object plugin uses — vim-surround, targets.vim, vim-textobj-* — and it's why operator-pending shows up in Vim's `:help map-modes` matrix as its own column (`o`). Full treatment in [`text-objects.op-pending`](#text-objects.op-pending).

If you can answer "what's the verb? what's the noun?" for a command you want to run, you can usually type it. That's the test for whether you've internalized the grammar — and once you have, every new motion or text object is a free upgrade for *every* operator.
See [`appendix.grammar-matrix`](#appendix.grammar-matrix) for the full operator × motion table, and [`appendix.synonyms`](#appendix.synonyms) for the complete list of single-key aliases.
